perturbation.original_text: 1. The Parties agree to abide by the following rules of non-circumvention and non-disclosure for a period of Two years from the effective date hereof. Such covenant and agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement for any reason whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. The Parties agree to abide by the following rules of non-circumvention and non-disclosure for a period of Ninety days from the effective date hereof. Such covenant and agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement for any reason whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-circumvention and non-disclosure period to 90 days may be unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws if it doesn't allow sufficient time to prevent misappropriation. Pennsylvania's trade secret law requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and 90 days may not be considered reasonable depending on the industry and the sensitivity of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania's PUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. A 90-day non-disclosure period might be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially if the information involved has a longer commercial lifespan or requires more time for reverse engineering or competitive exploitation. The modified text could lead to a claim that the agreement does not provide adequate protection, thus violating the spirit and intent of trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022) - Damages :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages. --Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages. --If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Pennsylvania may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the non-circumvention and non-disclosure period from two years to 90 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that this shorter period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Pennsylvania law, specifically the Pennsylvania Uniform Trade Secrets Act (PUTSA), which requires 'reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets.' Scraped Law Snippet 2, 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304, discusses damages for misappropriation of trade secrets, implying that trade secrets are legally protected and that misappropriation can result in damages. Reducing the protection period to 90 days could be seen as not providing adequate or reasonable protection, especially considering the typical lifespan or reverse-engineering time of sensitive information, potentially conflicting with the law's intent to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 12. This Agreement is a full recourse agreement concluded under the laws of Pennsylvania and said forum shall be applicable law covering the construction, interpretation, execution, validity, enforceability, performance, and any other such matters in respect to this Agreement, including any breach or claim of breach hereof. 13. This Agreement shall be governed by law and construed to be in accordance with the laws of the State of Pennsylvania applicable to contracts made and to be performed solely in such State by parties thereof. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be adjudicated in arbitration under the rules of the American Arbitration Association.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. This Agreement is a full recourse agreement concluded under the laws of Pennsylvania and said forum shall be applicable law covering the construction, interpretation, execution, validity, enforceability, performance, and any other such matters in respect to this Agreement, including any breach or claim of breach hereof. 13. This Agreement shall be governed by law and construed to be in accordance with the laws of the State of Pennsylvania applicable to contracts made and to be performed solely in such State by parties thereof. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be adjudicated in a court of equity, waiving the right to a jury trial.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing all disputes to be adjudicated in a court of equity and waiving the right to a jury trial can be problematic, as parties generally have a right to a jury trial for legal claims (as opposed to equitable claims). While parties can waive this right, the waiver must be knowing and voluntary. This clause removes the option entirely.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Pennsylvania law allows for the waiver of a jury trial, 42 Pa.C.S. § 5104 implies that such waiver must be an informed decision. The modified clause mandates the waiver by forcing adjudication in a court of equity, which effectively removes the choice. This could be challenged as an involuntary or uninformed waiver of a constitutional right.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5104 (2022) - Trial by jury :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 51 - Preliminary Provisions Section 5104 - Trial by jury Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 5104 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5104. Trial by jury. (a) General rule.-- Except where the right to trial by jury is enlarged by statute, trial by jury shall be as heretofore, and the right thereof shall remain inviolate. Trial by jury may be waived in the manner prescribed by general rules. (b) Civil verdicts.-- In any civil case a verdict rendered by at least five-sixths of the jury shall be the verdict of the jury and shall have the same effect as a unanimous verdict of the jury. (c) Criminal matters.-- In criminal cases the Commonwealth shall have the same right to trial by jury as does the accused. (Apr. 28, 1978, P.L.202, No.53, eff. 60 days) 1978 Amendment. Act 53 amended subsec. (a) and added subsec. (c). Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Pennsylvania may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates disputes be adjudicated in a court of equity, effectively waiving the right to a jury trial. The Law Explanation suggests this waiver could be challenged as involuntary or uninformed. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 42 Pa.C.S. § 5104, states that 'trial by jury shall be as heretofore, and the right thereof shall remain inviolate' but also says it 'may be waived in the manner prescribed by general rules.' By forcing adjudication in a court of equity, the changed text removes the option for a jury trial for legal claims, creating an inconsistency with the right to a jury trial as indicated in the snippet and explained in the Perturbation Explanation. This makes the waiver potentially non-compliant with the statute's requirement for how such waivers must occur.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Any written notice required or allowed to be given hereunder shall be deemed to have been duly and properly given and delivered (a) as of the date actually hand delivered to the Party to be charged with receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Any written notice required or allowed to be given hereunder shall be deemed to have been duly and properly given and delivered (a) only when sent via carrier pigeon.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting acceptable notice to 'carrier pigeon' is unreasonable and likely unenforceable, potentially conflicting with established principles of contract law that require commercially reasonable notice methods. This could be considered an attempt to avoid providing effective notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 204 emphasizes the need for good faith and fair dealing in contract performance, and recognizes supplementary terms. Specifying 'carrier pigeon' as the exclusive method of notice is patently unreasonable in modern commerce, and contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing and accepted usage in modern contracts, potentially invalidating the notice provision and leading to legal challenges regarding its enforceability. Pennsylvania adheres to Restatement of Contracts.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a general and commercially reasonable method of notice with an absurd and impractical method ('carrier pigeon'). This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract performance, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, suggesting a party is attempting to avoid providing effective notice. While the scraped snippet is not directly relevant, the legal explanation and the drastic change in the method of notice sufficiently demonstrate a departure from accepted practices and a potential attempt to undermine the purpose of the notice provision.

perturbation.original_text: 4. In the event of circumvention by the "Parties" involved in this transaction, either directly or indirectly, it is agreed and guaranteed that a monetary penalty will be paid by the person or persons engaged in or circumvention. This payment will additionally include all reasonable legal expenses incurred by the aggrieved party.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. In the event of circumvention by the "Parties" involved in this transaction, either directly or indirectly, it is agreed and guaranteed that a monetary penalty of $10,000,000 will be paid by the person or persons engaged in or circumvention. This payment will additionally include all legal expenses incurred by the circumvented party, regardless of their reasonableness.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying a fixed penalty of $10,000,000 without regard to actual damages could be deemed an unenforceable penalty under Pennsylvania law, particularly if it is disproportionate to the harm caused by the breach. Additionally, requiring payment of all legal expenses, regardless of reasonableness, may also be challenged as against public policy.
perturbation.law_citation: Pennsylvania law on liquidated damages (see case law such as  Holt's Cigar Co. v. 222 Liberty Associates, 404 Pa. Super. 578, 591 A.2d 743 (1991))
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law disallows penalties in contracts; liquidated damages must be a reasonable forecast of actual damages. A fixed $10,000,000 penalty for circumvention, without relation to actual harm, is likely an unenforceable penalty. Holt's Cigar Co. v. 222 Liberty Associates clarifies the requirements for enforceable liquidated damages provisions, emphasizing the need for reasonableness. Furthermore, promising ALL legal expenses, regardless of their reasonableness is against public policy.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a fixed penalty of $10,000,000 for circumvention, which may not be proportional to the actual damages incurred, and mandates the payment of all legal expenses, regardless of their reasonableness. This contradicts the principle that liquidated damages should be a reasonable forecast of actual damages. The Law Explanation states, 'Pennsylvania law disallows penalties in contracts; liquidated damages must be a reasonable forecast of actual damages,' which indicates a contradiction between the fixed penalty and this requirement.
